bytes.topic: Raspberry Pi — Linux on embedded, GPIO via Python/C, choosing Pi vs microcontroller
bytes.title: Arduino vs Raspberry Pi: Choosing Between Linux and Microcontroller
bytes.body: When deciding between Arduino and Raspberry Pi, consider the trade-offs between Linux and microcontroller simplicity. Arduino boards run on a lightweight C++ framework, ideal for rapid prototyping and IoT projects. In contrast, Raspberry Pi offers a full Linux distribution, enabling advanced development and customization. However, this comes at the cost of increased complexity and resource overhead. If your project requires intricate low-level control or real-time performance, Arduino's microcontroller-based approach may be more suitable. Conversely, if you need to leverage Linux's versatility and extensive libraries, Raspberry Pi might be the better choice.

bytes.topic: Embedded Fundamentals — microcontroller vs microprocessor, bare metal vs RTOS
bytes.title: Arduino - Microcontroller vs Microprocessor: Choosing the Right One for Your Project
bytes.body: Arduino boards are based on microcontrollers, which integrate the processor, memory, and peripherals on a single chip. This design provides low power consumption, reduced size, and increased reliability. In contrast, microprocessors are separate components that require external memory and peripherals. When working with Arduino, you'll typically use a microcontroller-based board. To illustrate the difference, consider the Arduino Uno, which features the ATmega328P microcontroller. This chip includes 32KB of flash memory, 2KB of SRAM, and various peripherals like timers and serial communication interfaces. For projects requiring advanced multitasking and real-time operating systems (RTOS), you might opt for an Arduino Due, which uses a microprocessor-based SAM3X8E chip.

bytes.topic: Peripheral Communication — reading sensors, driving actuators, protocol selection
bytes.title: Arduino: Mastering Analog and Digital I/O with Sensors and Actuators
bytes.body: When reading analog sensors, use `analogRead()` to convert ADC values to voltage, and `map()` to linearly scale values. For digital sensors, employ `digitalRead()` and `pinMode()` to set input modes. To drive actuators, utilize `analogWrite()` for PWM outputs and `digitalWrite()` for digital outputs. The choice between `I2C`, `SPI`, and `UART` protocols depends on the sensor or actuator's requirements. For instance, use `Wire` for I2C communication.

bytes.topic: Choosing a Platform — Arduino vs ESP32 vs Raspberry Pi vs STM32 decision framework
bytes.title: Arduino vs ESP32 vs Raspberry Pi vs STM32: Choosing the Right Microcontroller for Your Project
bytes.body: When selecting a microcontroller for your Arduino project, consider factors like processing power, memory, and I/O capabilities. Arduino boards offer a range of options, but you may need more power or specific features. ESP32 combines Wi-Fi and Bluetooth capabilities, while Raspberry Pi provides a Linux-based OS. STM32 series offer high-performance and large Flash memory. Evaluate your project's requirements and choose the board that best fits. For instance, if you need Wi-Fi and BLE connectivity, ESP32 might be the best choice.

bytes.topic: Bootloaders — what they do, OTA update mechanisms, secure boot
bytes.title: Arduino Bootloaders 101: OTA Updates and Secure Boot
bytes.body: Arduino boards rely on bootloaders to facilitate Over-The-Air (OTA) firmware updates. The Arduino IDE uses the `AVR109` protocol for uploading sketches to the board. However, for OTA updates, we use the `AVR109` bootloader alongside libraries like `WiFiClient` and `ArduinoOTA`. Secure boot is achieved through the `MK20DX256` bootloader's secure flash area, which stores the boot code. You can use `arduino-esp32` library to implement secure boot on ESP32 boards.

bytes.topic: Safety-Critical Systems — MISRA C, functional safety, certification considerations
bytes.title: Arduino Safety-Critical Systems: MISRA C Compliance and Certification
bytes.body:
Arduino boards running safety-critical systems must adhere to MISRA C guidelines to ensure functional safety. The Arduino IDE provides a MISRA C 2012 compliance mode, which can be enabled by setting the `compiler.misra2012` flag to `true` in the `platformio.ini` file. To write MISRA C compliant code, use the `Arduino::MISRA::_Static_assert` macro to verify integer overflow checks. For example:
```cpp
Arduino::MISRA::_Static_assert(sizeof(int) == 2, "Error: sizeof(int) not 2");
```
Certification for safety-critical systems typically requires a thorough audit of the code, including code reviews and static analysis. Arduino boards may be certified to relevant safety standards such as IEC 61508 or ISO 13849.
